english: 1. Ssk. माष, pea, Phaseolus radiatus, = mon-sráṅ (mon srang) or greu (gre'u) Wdn. -- 2. W. the contrary of ya-śa (ya sha), contempt, scorn, disregard. -- 3. W. trigger of a musket.
Ssk. माषक, Cs.: a small gold weight and coin in ancient India.
name formed from the Hebrew מָשִׁיחַ, for Christ, the Greek word not being adapted to the Tib. language Chr. Prot.

Ssk. great, used in names and titles: ma-hā-kā-la (ma hA kA la) and de-ba (de ba) = Siva Glr.; ma-hā-tsī-na (ma hA tsI na), ma-hā-tsin (ma hA tsin) the modern name of China, formerly rgya-nág (rgya nag); ma-hā-tsi-nai skad (ma hA tsI na'i skad) the Chinese language Wdk.; ma-hā-rā-dzā (ma hA rA dzA) the great king, title of some princes, particularly that of Kashmere.
Ssk. महिष, buffalo Glr., ma-he-mo (ma he mo) female of it.
1. son-in-law Dzl., mag-skud (mag skud) son-in-law and father-in-law Dom. 2. bridegroom col.
1. C. col. for mi oṅ (mi 'ong), mi ḍug (mi 'dug) (?); so also in some passages of the Ma. -- 2. v. máṅ-po (mang po).

1. much, many, mi maṅ-po (rnams) (mi mang po rnams) many people, also (like οἱ πολλοί) most people, the gross or bulk of the people, for which W. *máṅ-če* (*mang che*), e.g. *máṅ-če zer ḍug* (*mang che zer 'dug*) most people say, or, mostly it is said etc.; ḥkhor máṅ-po (rnams) ('khor mang po rnams) the numerous retinue Dzl.; máṅ-por (mang por) adverb mostly (not frq.) Zam.; čhu maṅ-nyuṅ ltos (chu mang nyung ltos) look after the height of the water, whether there is much or little of it; gčig bsgyúr-ba-la maṅ-nyuṅ med (gcig bsgyur ba la mang nyung med) if you multiply by 1, you will get neither more nor less Wdk. -- 2. very, very much, with verbs, chiefly col., maṅ-po ḥjigs (mang po 'jigs) I am very much afraid. Comp. and deriv. maṅ-bkúr (mang bkur) = máṅ-pos bkúr-ba (mang pos bkur ba) v. bkúr-ba (bkur ba) I. and II. -- maṅ-gé-mo (mang ge mo) long ago, long since (?) Cs. -- *maṅ-ṅa* (*mang nga*) W. col. for máṅ-por (mang por), máṅ-ba(r) (mang ba(r)); *žag daṅ žag maṅ-ṅa maṅ-ṅa táṅ-če* (*zhag dang zhag mang nga mang nga tang che*) to give a little more every day. -- máṅ-če (mang che) v. above. -- máṅ-ja (mang ja) a liberal distribution of tea Ld.-Glr. Schl. fol. 27, a, and p. 72. máṅ-du (mang du) is not only the termin. case, but also a compound of maṅ (mang) and the synon. du (du), being used exactly like máṅ-po (mang po), both in the nomin. and accus case, gdams-ṅág máṅ-du bstán-pas (gdams ngag mang du bstan pas) ḥbrás-bu bži ṭób-pa máṅ-du byuṅ ('bras bu bzhi thob pa mang du byung) as he gave manifold instructions, many became obtainers of the four fruits Tar. 14, 3.

I. vb. pf. maṅs (mangs), to be much, ḥdi máṅ-ṅam de maṅ ('di mang ngam de mang) is this much or that? i.e. which is more, this or that? Dzl.; dgra máṅs-pas (dgra mangs pas) as the enemies had become very numerous Dzl.; sman-dpyád máṅs-pas pán-rgyu med (sman dpyad mangs pas phan rgyu med) by making much of medical treatment he will not grow well Mil.; ma maṅ čig (ma mang cig) be it not much, let it not grow too much Mil. and elsewh.; máṅs-kyis ḍógs-pa (mangs kyis dogs pa) fearing lest it should grow too much Wdn. -- II. adj. 1. máṅ-po (mang po). -- 2. having much, bu maṅ-bar gyúr-ba (bu mang bar gyur ba) to get many children, bu-máṅs (bu mangs) rich in children Pth. -- máṅs-tsig (mangs tshig) a sign of the plural number, e.g. dag (dag) Gram. -- III. also sbst. plenty.
a province of Tibet bordering on Nepal, in which skyid-groṅ (skyid grong) is situated, v. skyid (skyid).
S.g. a mineral medicine; perh. man-dza-ri (man dza ri) Ssk. pearl.
Ssk, prop. Tib. dkyil-ḥkhor (dkyil 'khor) jewels, viands etc. presented as offerings, and arranged in a cirle Glr. and elsewhere, cf. tsogs (tshogs).
1. = nad (nad) (?) lus mad-méd-čiṅ (lus mad med cing) Sambh. -- 2. sometimes for smad (smad).

true, kyed mad gsúṅ-žiṅ (khyed mad gsung zhing) as you speak what is true Mil.; ma nyés-pai bden-tsig mád-po smras kyaṅ (ma nyes pa'i bden tshig mad po smras kyang) although he solemnly declared not to have committed it Pth.
I. sbst., also máṅ-na (mang na), má-na (ma na) Hind. a 'man' or Indian hundredweight, equal to about 80 pounds, anglicized maund. -- II W. for ma yin (ma yin) (B. min (min)) 1. it is not;
